ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai crispy fish noodle soup. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Thai crispy fish noodle soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Thai noodle soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of crispy fish noodle soup weighing approximately 450 g, comprising rice noodles, crispy fried fish pieces, and clear fish broth with aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Fish Noodle Soup — nutrient profile estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia (2020) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for rice noodles (cooked), fried fish, and fish broth components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai crispy fish noodle soup as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 55% cooked rice noodles (USDA FDC #169730), 20% deep-fried fish fillet (FAO/INFOODS SEA), 20% clear fish broth (USDA FDC #173580), and 5% aromatics and condiments (fish sauce, garlic, spring onion). Per-100g values represent the blended dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and analogous noodle soup entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai crispy fish noodle soup. GI of 53 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (cooked, GI ~53, low category) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source in this dish. The addition of protein (fish) and fat (frying oil) is expected to moderate postprandial glucose response, supporting the low-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 55.8) / 100 ≈ 29.6, rounded to 29.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1890 mg per bowl), this dish contributes a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce and consume the broth sparingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 53 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 29 per bowl, this dish poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from deep-fried fish (approximately 0.7 g saturated fat per 100 g) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose steamed or poached fish variants where available, and avoid consuming the full broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fish Noodle Soup (Thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวปลาทอดกรอบน้ำ, romanized: Kuay Tiao Pla Thod Krob Nam) is a Thai noodle soup dish featuring deep-fried crispy fish pieces served over rice noodles in a clear, aromatic fish broth. The dish is a staple of Thai noodle cuisine and is commonly found at hawker stalls and Thai restaurants across Thailand and Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The broth is typically prepared by simmering fish bones with garlic, coriander root, and white pepper, producing a light yet flavourful soup base. The fish — commonly snakehead fish, tilapia, or sea bass — is coated lightly and deep-fried until golden and crunchy, providing a textural contrast to the soft rice noodles and clear broth. Garnishes typically include bean sprouts, spring onion, coriander leaves, and fried garlic. Condiments such as fish sauce, sugar, dried chilli flakes, and white pepper vinegar are served on the side, allowing diners to adjust seasoning to taste. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal providing approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution from the fish (approximately 6.2 g per 100 g) and a carbohydrate base from rice noodles (approximately 12.4 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), making it a relatively moderate glycemic option among noodle dishes. However, sodium content is high due to fish sauce and broth, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-compliant when prepared with fish sauce free of non-halal additives.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai crispy fish noodle soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the constituent ingredients. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles as the dominant glycemic component. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component entries used for estimation. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA entries used for the fish component. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed negligible/trace for fish and rice noodle components per USDA SR Legacy — confirmed zero per component source data. 5. The input description references 'crispy chicken' which appears to be a menu description error; this record is processed as crispy fish noodle soup consistent with the food_name and cuisine context. 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 7. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate for a standard Thai restaurant bowl; actual restaurant portion may vary. 8. Sodium content is high and represents the primary clinical concern for this dish.
